@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/augmentative and alternative communication / social communication / pragmatic language / relationship building/social vocabulary in aac / pragmatic functions / conversational vocabulary / emotional expression/9-materials-that-help-aac-for-social-words#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my son can use his aac device to ask for things. he's gotten really good at it, actually — the result of months and months of work, modeling, practice, and patience. he can request food when he's hungry, specific toys when he wants to play, particular activities when he's bored. he can tell us when he doesn't want something — 'no,' 'stop,' 'all done,' 'different.' he can ask for help when he needs it. he can tell us 'more' when he wants something to continue. this is huge. this is life-changing. before aac, he would have meltdowns trying to communicate these things. now he has words for his basic needs, and our family life is transformed. but something is missing. something that only becomes visible when you watch him in moments that aren't about needs at all. last week, his grandmother visited after being away for three months. she lives far away and video calls just aren't the same. she walked through the door and his face lit up with pure, uncontainable joy. he ran to hug her — threw his whole body into her arms. the reunion was real and beautiful. and then... nothing. he had no words to say what was clearly in his heart. he couldn't say 'i missed you so much.' he couldn't say 'grandma, i'm so happy you're here.' he couldn't tell her about all the things that happened while she was gone — the trip to the beach, his new teacher, the funny thing the dog did. he could ask her for juice. he could ask her to play. but he couldn't tell her he loved her. i watched the moment slip away, the things unsaid filling the room. i see the same thing at the park. other kids are laughing at something — some shared joke, some silly thing that happened. my son is laughing too. he gets it. he's part of the moment socially but not communicatively. he understands the humor but he can't add to it. he can't say 'that's so funny' or 'do it again' or 'remember when' or contribute his own observations. he could request the swing. but he couldn't share the experience of swinging, couldn't say 'whee!' or 'look at me!' or 'i'm flying!' his aac is full of nouns. food items with pictures of bananas and crackers and pizza. toy names. places. some verbs — want, go, help, play, eat. this is the vocabulary of needs and wants. but where are the words for connection? where is 'guess what'? where is 'i know, right'? where is 'that's so cool' or 'that's weird' or 'i don't like it when' or 'i'm excited about' or 'remember when we'? where are the words that make you a friend instead of just someone who asks for things? where are the words that let you share yourself? we've built him a system for needs. and needs are important — food, drink, comfort, help. but humans need more than physical needs met. we need to belong. we need to share experiences. we need to express who we are — our personalities, our humor, our opinions, our feelings about the world. how do we give him the vocabulary for that? how do we move beyond 'i want' to 'i feel' and 'i think' and 'did you see that' and 'that reminds me of'? his device can speak thousands of words. but it doesn't speak the language of friendship. not yet?

acceptedAnswer.text: SOCIAL/PRAGMATIC VOCABULARY IN AAC / PRAGMATIC LANGUAGE FUNCTIONS / SOCIAL COMMUNICATION IN AAC USERS / RELATIONSHIP-BUILDING VOCABULARY! Social vocabulary in AAC refers to the words and phrases that enable communication beyond instrumental functions (requesting, rejecting, labeling) to include social functions (commenting, sharing experiences, expressing opinions, building relationships, maintaining conversations, expressing personality and emotion). Pragmatic language encompasses the social use of language — not just what we say but how, when, and why we say it for social purposes. Many AAC systems are initially developed around requesting vocabulary, which is essential but insufficient for full social participation. Social vocabulary includes words for: greetings and closings ('hi,' 'bye,' 'see you later'), comments and reactions ('wow,' 'cool,' 'uh oh,' 'that's funny'), conversational maintenance ('and then,' 'guess what,' 'know what,' 'I know'), emotional expression ('I feel,' 'I'm excited,' 'that made me sad'), opinions and preferences ('I think,' 'I like when,' 'the best part was'), social connection ('I missed you,' 'I love you,' 'let's do this together'), and narrative/sharing ('remember when,' 'one time,' 'did you see'). Without explicit attention to social vocabulary, AAC users may be able to get their needs met but remain excluded from the social fabric of conversation and relationship.

mainEntity.name: What is Social/Pragmatic Vocabulary in AAC / Pragmatic Language Functions / Social Communication in AAC Users / Relationship-Building Vocabulary?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social vocabulary in AAC refers to the words and phrases that enable communication beyond instrumental functions (requesting, rejecting, labeling) to include social functions (commenting, sharing experiences, expressing opinions, building relationships, maintaining conversations, expressing personality and emotion). Pragmatic language encompasses the social use of language — not just what we say but how, when, and why we say it for social purposes. Many AAC systems are initially developed around requesting vocabulary, which is essential but insufficient for full social participation. Social vocabulary includes words for: greetings and closings ('hi,' 'bye,' 'see you later'), comments and reactions ('wow,' 'cool,' 'uh oh,' 'that's funny'), conversational maintenance ('and then,' 'guess what,' 'know what,' 'I know'), emotional expression ('I feel,' 'I'm excited,' 'that made me sad'), opinions and preferences ('I think,' 'I like when,' 'the best part was'), social connection ('I missed you,' 'I love you,' 'let's do this together'), and narrative/sharing ('remember when,' 'one time,' 'did you see'). Without explicit attention to social vocabulary, AAC users may be able to get their needs met but remain excluded from the social fabric of conversation and relationship. Common signs include: AAC use limited primarily to requests and rejections, Child can request items but not comment on experiences, Limited vocabulary for expressing emotions or opinions, Difficulty initiating or maintaining back-and-forth conversation, Unable to share experiences or tell about events.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Comments and Reactions Core Board help with social?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social comments and reactions are the glue of conversation — the words that show you're listening, that you're engaged, that you have an opinion. Words like 'wow,' 'cool,' 'uh oh,' 'yay,' 'that's funny,' 'I like that,' 'that's weird,' 'no way' are constantly used by speaking peers but often missing from AAC vocabularies. A dedicated social comments board or page provides easy access to these high-frequency social words. Unlike requests (which lead to getting something), comments share experience and build connection. When a child can respond 'that's so cool!' to a peer's story, they become a conversation partner, not just a bystander. These words also express personality — the child who says 'awesome!' versus 'interesting' versus 'weird' is revealing their unique perspective. Social comments transform AAC from a tool for getting things to a tool for being someone. Price: ₹200–1,500. DIY option: Create a dedicated page or board for social reactions. Include: positive reactions (wow, cool, awesome, yay, I love it, that's great), negative reactions (uh oh, oh no, that's bad, I don't like it), humor responses (that's funny, haha, silly, weird), surprise (whoa, no way, really?), and interest (tell me more, and then what?, I want to see). Print, laminate, keep accessible during social times. Safety: Social comments should be modeled extensively — show the child how and when these words are used in natural conversation. Don't just add them to the device; use them yourself.

mainEntity.name: How does a Conversational Maintenance Vocabulary Cards help with social?

acceptedAnswer.text: Conversations are more than exchanging information — they're a dance of turns, prompts, responses, and flow. Conversational maintenance words keep the dialogue going: 'and then what?,' 'guess what,' 'know what?,' 'I know right?,' 'wait,' 'hold on,' 'one more thing,' 'that reminds me of,' 'speaking of that.' These words let a person take turns, build on topics, introduce new ideas, and signal engagement. Without them, AAC users often communicate in isolated statements rather than flowing conversation. They answer when asked but don't extend, elaborate, or redirect. Conversational maintenance vocabulary transforms communication from Q&A into genuine dialogue. It also gives the AAC user power in the conversation — the ability to hold the floor, change topics, or signal that they have more to say. Price: ₹150–1,000. DIY option: Create cards or an AAC page for conversation flow: topic starters ('guess what,' 'you know what,' 'I have something to tell you'), extenders ('and then,' 'also,' 'and also,' 'plus'), redirectors ('speaking of,' 'that reminds me,' 'oh, and'), turn holders ('wait,' 'hold on,' 'I'm not done'), and engagement signals ('really?,' 'tell me more,' 'then what?'). Practice these in natural conversation, modeling their use. Safety: These words are learned through use in real conversation, not drilling. Model them constantly in natural talk, showing when and why they're used.

mainEntity.name: How does a Emotion and Feeling Expression Vocabulary help with social?

acceptedAnswer.text: Humans don't just have needs — we have feelings. And feelings need words. Emotion vocabulary in AAC goes beyond 'happy,' 'sad,' 'mad' (the basics often provided) to include the full range of human emotional experience: 'excited,' 'nervous,' 'proud,' 'embarrassed,' 'frustrated,' 'confused,' 'surprised,' 'lonely,' 'grateful,' 'jealous,' 'overwhelmed,' 'calm,' 'silly.' It also includes words for expressing feelings about situations and people: 'I feel __ when,' 'that makes me feel,' 'I'm worried about,' 'I'm excited for.' Without rich emotion vocabulary, AAC users cannot fully express their inner experience, process emotions through communication, or receive appropriate emotional support. When a child can say 'I'm nervous about the doctor' instead of just crying, connection and support become possible. Emotion words also support emotional regulation — naming feelings is the first step to managing them. Price: ₹200–1,500. DIY option: Expand emotion vocabulary systematically: basic emotions (happy, sad, angry, scared), intensity variations (a little worried vs. very worried, irritated vs. furious), complex emotions (proud, embarrassed, jealous, grateful, confused), physical-emotional states (tired, sick, overwhelmed, calm), and feeling expressions ('I feel __ about,' 'it makes me feel,' '__ makes me happy'). Include visuals that show these emotions on faces and bodies. Model emotion words throughout the day. Safety: Don't wait for emotions to be present to teach emotion words. Model them narrating your own feelings, characters' feelings in books, and the child's observed emotional states.

mainEntity.name: How does a Friendship and Relationship Vocabulary Materials help with social?

acceptedAnswer.text: Relationships require specific vocabulary — words for connection, affection, shared history, and social bonds. 'I missed you,' 'I love you,' 'you're my friend,' 'I like playing with you,' 'want to play together?,' 'that was fun,' 'let's do that again,' 'I'm sorry,' 'are you okay?,' 'I was thinking about you.' These words build and maintain relationships. They express care, repair ruptures, invite connection, and acknowledge bonds. Many AAC systems lack this vocabulary entirely, leaving users able to request things from people but not to express feelings about people. Friendship vocabulary also includes social invitations ('want to come over?,' 'sit with me'), social observations ('you look happy,' 'did something happen?'), and shared memory references ('remember when we,' 'like last time'). With these words, AAC users can actively build and nurture friendships rather than passively receiving social attention. Price: ₹200–1,200. DIY option: Create dedicated friendship/relationship vocabulary: affection expressions ('I love you,' 'I like you,' 'you're the best,' 'I missed you'), social invitations ('want to play?,' 'sit with me,' 'come here,' 'let's go together'), shared experience words ('that was fun,' 'let's do that again,' 'remember when'), repair words ('I'm sorry,' 'are you okay?,' 'I didn't mean to'), and observation/care words ('you seem sad,' 'what happened?,' 'I was thinking about you'). Program these prominently and model them in natural relationship moments. Safety: Model relationship vocabulary during genuine relational moments — not as drills, but as natural expressions of connection. Show the child how these words work in real relationships.

mainEntity.name: How does a Opinion and Preference Expression Tools help with social?

acceptedAnswer.text: Having opinions is part of being human. Expressing them is part of being known. Opinion vocabulary includes 'I think,' 'I like,' 'I don't like,' 'my favorite is,' 'the best part was,' 'I prefer,' 'I agree,' 'I disagree,' 'I wonder,' 'in my opinion.' These words let a person share their perspective, not just their needs. When a child can say 'I think the blue one is better' or 'my favorite part was when the dog ran,' they're sharing themselves, not just requesting. Opinion vocabulary also supports participation in classroom discussions, family conversations, and peer debates. It's how we learn each other's personalities — through the opinions we express. Without opinion words, AAC users may be seen as having no preferences beyond immediate wants, when in fact they have rich inner perspectives waiting for vocabulary. Price: ₹150–1,000. DIY option: Build opinion vocabulary: perspective starters ('I think,' 'I believe,' 'in my opinion'), preference words ('I like,' 'I love,' 'I don't like,' 'I prefer,' 'my favorite'), evaluation words ('the best,' 'the worst,' 'better,' 'not as good'), agreement/disagreement ('I agree,' 'I don't agree,' 'me too,' 'I think differently'), and wonder/curiosity ('I wonder,' 'maybe,' 'what if'). Create opportunities to use these — choice discussions, book discussions, preference games. Safety: Validate all opinions expressed through AAC, even if you disagree. The child is practicing expressing themselves — the goal is expression, not agreement.

mainEntity.name: How does a Narrative and Story-Sharing Vocabulary help with social?

acceptedAnswer.text: Humans are storytelling creatures. We share our experiences, recount our days, tell about things that happened. Narrative vocabulary enables this: 'one time,' 'yesterday,' 'remember when,' 'and then,' 'first... then... finally,' 'the funny part was,' 'guess what happened,' 'at school today,' 'I want to tell you about.' Without narrative vocabulary, AAC users can't share their experiences, can't tell about their day, can't recount funny moments or important events. They live experiences but can't communicate them. Narrative vocabulary also supports literacy and academic learning — understanding and creating stories requires these connecting and sequencing words. When a child can say 'At school today, first we did art, then we had lunch, and the funny part was my friend spilled milk,' they're sharing their life. They're known. They belong. Price: ₹150–1,200. DIY option: Create narrative vocabulary supports: time markers ('today,' 'yesterday,' 'one time,' 'last week,' 'tomorrow'), sequence words ('first,' 'then,' 'next,' 'after that,' 'finally,' 'at the end'), story starters ('guess what,' 'you know what happened?,' 'I have to tell you'), highlight words ('the best part,' 'the funny thing,' 'the problem was,' 'the surprise was'), and setting words ('at school,' 'at home,' 'at the park,' 'in the car'). Practice with daily recounts — 'Tell me about your day' becomes possible with these words. Safety: Create genuine opportunities for narrative — ask about the child's day, experiences, and observations. Provide wait time for them to construct their stories.

@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. AAC is for full human communication, not just need fulfillment. Social vocabulary — the words for connecting, sharing, reacting, belonging — must be explicitly added and modeled. Requesting vocabulary meets needs; social vocabulary builds relationships. Both are essential. Without social vocabulary, AAC users are limited to transactions in a world that runs on connection.

Without explicit attention to social vocabulary, AAC users may be able to get their needs met but remain excluded from the social fabric of conversation and relationship. signOrSymptom.name: AAC use limited primarily to requests and rejections
Child can request items but not comment on experiences
Limited vocabulary for expressing emotions or opinions
Difficulty initiating or maintaining back-and-forth conversation
Unable to share experiences or tell about events
AAC device lacks words for social rituals (greetings, thanks, sorry)
Cannot express agreement, disagreement, or reactions
Struggles to participate in peer social interactions communicatively

citation.name: Research on Social Communication and Pragmatic Language in AAC
citation.description: Research demonstrates that AAC users often develop requesting vocabulary but have limited access to social vocabulary that enables commenting, sharing, relationship-building, and full pragmatic function. Social vocabulary must be explicitly added, modeled, and taught — it does not automatically emerge from requesting ability. Communication partner training in social interaction with AAC users significantly impacts social communication outcomes. Access to robust social vocabulary is essential for quality of life, peer relationships, and social inclusion.

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Social
@graph.description: Evidence-based materials for ages 2–18 years (and across the lifespan)

item.name: Social Comments and Reactions Core Board
item.description: Social comments and reactions are the glue of conversation — the words that show you're listening, that you're engaged, that you have an opinion. Words like 'wow,' 'cool,' 'uh oh,' 'yay,' 'that's funny,' 'I like that,' 'that's weird,' 'no way' are constantly used by speaking peers but often missing from AAC vocabularies. A dedicated social comments board or page provides easy access to these high-frequency social words. Unlike requests (which lead to getting something), comments share experience and build connection. When a child can respond 'that's so cool!' to a peer's story, they become a conversation partner, not just a bystander. These words also express personality — the child who says 'awesome!' versus 'interesting' versus 'weird' is revealing their unique perspective. Social comments transform AAC from a tool for getting things to a tool for being someone.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a dedicated page or board for social reactions. Include: positive reactions (wow, cool, awesome, yay, I love it, that's great), negative reactions (uh oh, oh no, that's bad, I don't like it), humor responses (that's funny, haha, silly, weird), surprise (whoa, no way, really?), and interest (tell me more, and then what?, I want to see). Print, laminate, keep accessible during social times.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Social comments should be modeled extensively — show the child how and when these words are used in natural conversation. Don't just add them to the device; use them yourself.

item.name: Conversational Maintenance Vocabulary Cards
item.description: Conversations are more than exchanging information — they're a dance of turns, prompts, responses, and flow. Conversational maintenance words keep the dialogue going: 'and then what?,' 'guess what,' 'know what?,' 'I know right?,' 'wait,' 'hold on,' 'one more thing,' 'that reminds me of,' 'speaking of that.' These words let a person take turns, build on topics, introduce new ideas, and signal engagement. Without them, AAC users often communicate in isolated statements rather than flowing conversation. They answer when asked but don't extend, elaborate, or redirect. Conversational maintenance vocabulary transforms communication from Q&A into genuine dialogue. It also gives the AAC user power in the conversation — the ability to hold the floor, change topics, or signal that they have more to say.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create cards or an AAC page for conversation flow: topic starters ('guess what,' 'you know what,' 'I have something to tell you'), extenders ('and then,' 'also,' 'and also,' 'plus'), redirectors ('speaking of,' 'that reminds me,' 'oh, and'), turn holders ('wait,' 'hold on,' 'I'm not done'), and engagement signals ('really?,' 'tell me more,' 'then what?'). Practice these in natural conversation, modeling their use.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: These words are learned through use in real conversation, not drilling. Model them constantly in natural talk, showing when and why they're used.

item.name: Emotion and Feeling Expression Vocabulary
item.description: Humans don't just have needs — we have feelings. And feelings need words. Emotion vocabulary in AAC goes beyond 'happy,' 'sad,' 'mad' (the basics often provided) to include the full range of human emotional experience: 'excited,' 'nervous,' 'proud,' 'embarrassed,' 'frustrated,' 'confused,' 'surprised,' 'lonely,' 'grateful,' 'jealous,' 'overwhelmed,' 'calm,' 'silly.' It also includes words for expressing feelings about situations and people: 'I feel __ when,' 'that makes me feel,' 'I'm worried about,' 'I'm excited for.' Without rich emotion vocabulary, AAC users cannot fully express their inner experience, process emotions through communication, or receive appropriate emotional support. When a child can say 'I'm nervous about the doctor' instead of just crying, connection and support become possible. Emotion words also support emotional regulation — naming feelings is the first step to managing them.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Expand emotion vocabulary systematically: basic emotions (happy, sad, angry, scared), intensity variations (a little worried vs. very worried, irritated vs. furious), complex emotions (proud, embarrassed, jealous, grateful, confused), physical-emotional states (tired, sick, overwhelmed, calm), and feeling expressions ('I feel __ about,' 'it makes me feel,' '__ makes me happy'). Include visuals that show these emotions on faces and bodies. Model emotion words throughout the day.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Don't wait for emotions to be present to teach emotion words. Model them narrating your own feelings, characters' feelings in books, and the child's observed emotional states.

item.name: Friendship and Relationship Vocabulary Materials
item.description: Relationships require specific vocabulary — words for connection, affection, shared history, and social bonds. 'I missed you,' 'I love you,' 'you're my friend,' 'I like playing with you,' 'want to play together?,' 'that was fun,' 'let's do that again,' 'I'm sorry,' 'are you okay?,' 'I was thinking about you.' These words build and maintain relationships. They express care, repair ruptures, invite connection, and acknowledge bonds. Many AAC systems lack this vocabulary entirely, leaving users able to request things from people but not to express feelings about people. Friendship vocabulary also includes social invitations ('want to come over?,' 'sit with me'), social observations ('you look happy,' 'did something happen?'), and shared memory references ('remember when we,' 'like last time'). With these words, AAC users can actively build and nurture friendships rather than passively receiving social attention.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create dedicated friendship/relationship vocabulary: affection expressions ('I love you,' 'I like you,' 'you're the best,' 'I missed you'), social invitations ('want to play?,' 'sit with me,' 'come here,' 'let's go together'), shared experience words ('that was fun,' 'let's do that again,' 'remember when'), repair words ('I'm sorry,' 'are you okay?,' 'I didn't mean to'), and observation/care words ('you seem sad,' 'what happened?,' 'I was thinking about you'). Program these prominently and model them in natural relationship moments.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Model relationship vocabulary during genuine relational moments — not as drills, but as natural expressions of connection. Show the child how these words work in real relationships.

item.name: Opinion and Preference Expression Tools
item.description: Having opinions is part of being human. Expressing them is part of being known. Opinion vocabulary includes 'I think,' 'I like,' 'I don't like,' 'my favorite is,' 'the best part was,' 'I prefer,' 'I agree,' 'I disagree,' 'I wonder,' 'in my opinion.' These words let a person share their perspective, not just their needs. When a child can say 'I think the blue one is better' or 'my favorite part was when the dog ran,' they're sharing themselves, not just requesting. Opinion vocabulary also supports participation in classroom discussions, family conversations, and peer debates. It's how we learn each other's personalities — through the opinions we express. Without opinion words, AAC users may be seen as having no preferences beyond immediate wants, when in fact they have rich inner perspectives waiting for vocabulary.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Build opinion vocabulary: perspective starters ('I think,' 'I believe,' 'in my opinion'), preference words ('I like,' 'I love,' 'I don't like,' 'I prefer,' 'my favorite'), evaluation words ('the best,' 'the worst,' 'better,' 'not as good'), agreement/disagreement ('I agree,' 'I don't agree,' 'me too,' 'I think differently'), and wonder/curiosity ('I wonder,' 'maybe,' 'what if'). Create opportunities to use these — choice discussions, book discussions, preference games.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Validate all opinions expressed through AAC, even if you disagree. The child is practicing expressing themselves — the goal is expression, not agreement.

item.name: Narrative and Story-Sharing Vocabulary
item.description: Humans are storytelling creatures. We share our experiences, recount our days, tell about things that happened. Narrative vocabulary enables this: 'one time,' 'yesterday,' 'remember when,' 'and then,' 'first... then... finally,' 'the funny part was,' 'guess what happened,' 'at school today,' 'I want to tell you about.' Without narrative vocabulary, AAC users can't share their experiences, can't tell about their day, can't recount funny moments or important events. They live experiences but can't communicate them. Narrative vocabulary also supports literacy and academic learning — understanding and creating stories requires these connecting and sequencing words. When a child can say 'At school today, first we did art, then we had lunch, and the funny part was my friend spilled milk,' they're sharing their life. They're known. They belong.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create narrative vocabulary supports: time markers ('today,' 'yesterday,' 'one time,' 'last week,' 'tomorrow'), sequence words ('first,' 'then,' 'next,' 'after that,' 'finally,' 'at the end'), story starters ('guess what,' 'you know what happened?,' 'I have to tell you'), highlight words ('the best part,' 'the funny thing,' 'the problem was,' 'the surprise was'), and setting words ('at school,' 'at home,' 'at the park,' 'in the car'). Practice with daily recounts — 'Tell me about your day' becomes possible with these words.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Create genuine opportunities for narrative — ask about the child's day, experiences, and observations. Provide wait time for them to construct their stories.

item.name: Question Words for Social Curiosity
item.description: Asking questions is a powerful social skill. It shows interest in others, drives conversation, and builds relationships. Social question vocabulary includes 'what happened?,' 'how was your day?,' 'are you okay?,' 'what do you think?,' 'did you see?,' 'where did you go?,' 'who was there?,' 'why did that happen?,' 'what's your favorite?' Questions directed at others show care and interest — they make the other person feel valued. Many AAC systems have question words (who, what, where, when, why) but not the social question phrases that combine them into relationship-building inquiries. When a child can ask a peer 'What did you do this weekend?' or say to a parent 'How was your day?,' they become an active participant in social reciprocity. They're not just answering — they're engaging.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create social question vocabulary: interest questions ('what happened?,' 'how was it?,' 'did you like it?,' 'what's that?'), care questions ('are you okay?,' 'do you need help?,' 'what's wrong?'), preference questions ('what's your favorite?,' 'what do you like?,' 'what do you want to do?'), experience questions ('where did you go?,' 'who was there?,' 'what did you do?'), and opinion questions ('what do you think?,' 'do you agree?'). Model asking these questions yourself, showing how they work in conversation.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Teach question-asking in natural contexts. Create situations where asking questions makes sense and practice the AAC vocabulary for those moments.

item.name: Humor and Playfulness Vocabulary
item.description: Humor is a uniquely human connection point. Laughing together bonds people. Humor vocabulary includes 'just kidding,' 'that's funny,' 'that's silly,' 'I'm joking,' 'funny face,' 'do it again,' 'watch this,' 'made you laugh,' 'knock knock,' 'silly me,' 'oops,' 'gotcha!' These words allow AAC users to participate in humor — not just laugh at jokes but make them, respond to them, and share in playful moments. Humor vocabulary also supports a child's ability to be playful and lighthearted, to show their personality through silliness, and to navigate the social world of teasing and joking. When a child can say 'just kidding!' or 'gotcha!' they're participating in social play, not just observing it. Humor vocabulary is often entirely absent from AAC systems, leaving users unable to express this fundamental part of social interaction.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Add humor vocabulary: playful markers ('just kidding,' 'I'm joking,' 'not really,' 'fooled you,' 'gotcha'), reaction words ('that's funny,' 'that's silly,' 'haha,' 'you're funny'), playful requests ('do it again,' 'watch this,' 'funny face,' 'be silly'), joke starters ('knock knock,' 'guess what,' 'want to hear something funny'), and recovery words ('oops,' 'my bad,' 'silly me'). Model playful use of these words, creating opportunities for shared humor.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Humor is culturally and contextually specific. Teach when humor is appropriate and how to read responses. Model recovery when jokes don't land.

item.name: Social Modeling Videos and Partner Training
item.description: Social vocabulary only works if it's used — by the AAC user and by the communication partners around them. Social modeling videos show AAC being used in real social situations: commenting, reacting, asking questions, sharing stories, making jokes. Partner training teaches family members, teachers, and peers how to create opportunities for social communication, how to respond to social AAC use, and how to model social vocabulary themselves. Without explicit training, communication partners often only interact with AAC users for functional purposes (requests, directions) rather than social purposes (chatting, sharing, connecting). Social modeling videos can also be used by the AAC user as self-models — watching examples of social communication they can replicate. Research shows that partner training is the strongest predictor of AAC success across all domains, including social.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create home videos of family members using social vocabulary on the child's AAC system — commenting, asking questions, sharing reactions. Watch these with the child as models. Train all communication partners (parents, grandparents, siblings, teachers, peers) on how to interact socially with AAC, not just functionally. Online resources (AssistiveWare, PrAACtical AAC, AAC Language Lab) offer free training content.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Social communication requires social context. Training materials should create real opportunities for social interaction, not just drills.

socialMedia.caption: He can ask for juice. He can't say 'I love you.' When AAC handles requests but not relationships, social vocabulary opens the door to connection. Here are 9 materials: social comments boards, conversation maintenance vocabulary, emotion expression words, friendship vocabulary, opinion tools, narrative language, social questions, humor vocabulary, and modeling/training resources. AAC is not just for getting things — it's for being someone. Save for your social vocabulary journey! 🔖 #AAC #SocialVocabularyAAC #PinnacleBlooms
